srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3a3d821/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F22%2F31f69acf46a0af2526c862d74036%2Fseedbank-2602240039.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7082bda/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F22%2F31f69acf46a0af2526c862d74036%2Fseedbank-2602240039.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3a3d821/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F22%2F31f69acf46a0af2526c862d74036%2Fseedbank-2602240039.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Marty Landorf, a volunteer, at the Chicago Botanic Gardens in Highland Park Illinois works on separating the seeds from the chaff at the seed bank in the Carr Administrative Center on February 10, 2026. | Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“If something isn't supported on a national level, then it becomes incumbent on states and regions to do that kind of work,” Havens said. “So that's why we're focusing right now in the Midwest.”</p><p>The network is the first of its kind in the Midwest, though similar initiatives have been active elsewhere in the country for years. Today, there are more than 25<a class="Link" href="https://appliedeco.org/nativeseednetwork/nsn-directory/" target="_blank" ><u> similar networks operating across the U.S</u></a>. In the western United States, these coalitions have come together in response to post-wildfire restoration projects.</p><p>“One of the reasons why we were among the first is because of this federal land ownership that we have in the West, whereas in the Midwest, it's more private land,” said Elizabeth Lager, a professor at the University of Nevada in Reno and co-founder of the Nevada Native Seed Partnership. More than 90 percent of all federal land is located in 11 western states.</p><p>Kramer said she hopes to run the seed availability survey again in 20 years and get a different response.</p><p>“I want them to say, ‘We have access to all the seed we need,’” said Kramer. “And we can move on to the next challenging question, like, ‘Why isn't the seed establishing in my restoration? Or, how do we manage the next challenge coming with climate change?’”</p>
entries.author: Juanpablo Ramirez-Franco | WBEZ

entries.title: CTA suffers most violent attacks in 24 years as Trump threatens $50 million funding cut over crime
entries.summary: <p>Aggravated assaults and batteries on the Chicago Transit Authority reached at least a 24-year high in 2025 as the Trump administration threatened to cut millions of dollars in funding after a series of jarring attacks.</p><p>The upward trend has continued into 2026, with those crimes climbing 33% over the same period last year, according to city data dating back to 2001.</p><p>It's bad news for the CTA and its interim leader, Nora Leerhsen, who's trying to highlight positive crime trends on the transit system and secure $50 million in grant funding from the federal government.</p><p>Unsatisfied with the CTA’s decision in December to boost the number of officers patrolling Chicago’s trains and buses, the Federal Transit Administration has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/12/19/president-trump-chicago-cta-crime-security-funding-cut-mayor-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" >threatened to withhold the money</a> if the CTA doesn't enact an acceptable safety plan by mid-March.</p><p>The CTA has touted an overall drop in crime, due in part to falling reports of robberies and thefts. But the record number of felony assaults and batteries underscores the work still needed to improve the CTA's image and appease the feds.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://flo.uri.sh/visualisation/27781479/embed" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" height="575" width="700" style="width:100%;" title="Interactive or visual content"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>The perception of transit safety was tarnished further in November, when 26-year-old Bethany MaGee was set ablaze on a Blue Line train in the Loop — an attack that drew the ire of President Donald Trump.</p><p>Federal prosecutors have <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/11/19/terrorism-charges-filed-after-woman-lit-on-fire-on-blue-line-in-loop" target="_blank" >charged Lawrence Reed</a> with terrorism, but the Chicago Police Department logged the crime as an aggravated battery.</p><p>Crimes are considered "aggravated" when a perpetrator uses a weapon or causes serious injury, and they can be prosecuted as felonies. That’s in contrast to "simple" batteries and assaults, which are misdemeanors.</p><p>Leerhsen has said she's taking security seriously and working with the police to address concerns.</p><p>“When I speak with riders and employees, [safety] is the issue raised most,” <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/01/22/cta-crime-police-officer-transit-rider-interaction-program" target="_blank" >Leerhsen told the City Club of Chicago</a> in January. “As a daily rider — and I've been on the system over 500 times just in 2025, and as a woman and a mother who often rides with her two young children — it's personal to me.</p><p>"While overall crime is down, we can't and won't stop doing so much more.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b40001" name="image-b40001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe0d18a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fda%2F524c5fd24ef49d2e351b3fe1f183%2Fctacityclub-260123-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/53ace34/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fda%2F524c5fd24ef49d2e351b3fe1f183%2Fctacityclub-260123-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8da468f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fda%2F524c5fd24ef49d2e351b3fe1f183%2Fctacityclub-260123-10.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b63dedd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2Fda%2F524c5fd24ef49d2e351b3fe1f183%2Fctacityclub-260123-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Acting CTA President Nora Leerhsen speaks at a City Club Chicago on Jan. 22.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘A big change’</h3><p>Attacks on the CTA rose last year, even as those crimes fell 15% citywide between 2024 and 2025 — the largest drop in a decade.</p><p>The police department recorded 469 aggravated assaults and batteries on the CTA last year, above the 441 reported in 2024, city data shows. A new record for those crimes has been set every year since 2021.</p><p>“It’s definitely a big change,” said David Olson, co-director of the Center for Criminal Justice Research at Loyola University Chicago, who has analyzed the jump in aggravated batteries and assaults on CTA trains, platforms and buses.</p><p>Olson said the rate of violent crime on transit remains statistically low. Most aggravated batteries and assaults in Chicago happen outside of public transit, and those crimes often involve people who know each other, he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/38b59af/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4527x2541+0+192/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F29%2F2fbe7a4ee288dc0f0456e2bca8fe%2F160205-david-olson.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d5e358/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4527x2541+0+192/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F29%2F2fbe7a4ee288dc0f0456e2bca8fe%2F160205-david-olson.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="David Olson, an associate professor in the Department of Criminal Justice and Criminology at Loyola University Chicago." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d5e358/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4527x2541+0+192/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F29%2F2fbe7a4ee288dc0f0456e2bca8fe%2F160205-david-olson.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/73ea67b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4527x2541+0+192/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F29%2F2fbe7a4ee288dc0f0456e2bca8fe%2F160205-david-olson.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d5e358/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4527x2541+0+192/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F29%2F2fbe7a4ee288dc0f0456e2bca8fe%2F160205-david-olson.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>David Olson, co-director of the Center for Criminal Justice Research at Loyola University Chicago</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Loyola University Chicago</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>His analysis found that more CTA-related aggravated batteries and assaults are happening in the Loop, accounting for around 16% in 2025, compared to about 5% of those crimes in 2009.</p><p>The train stops that had the most aggravated attacks in 2025 were Clark/Lake and the Red Line stops at 63rd and Lake streets. More than half of the attacks happened on trains and buses.</p><p>Transit crime numbers may be fodder for the Trump administration's attempts to bully Chicago officials, but Olson said it's important to consider that most of these crimes are not random.</p><p>“Every crime is not what you see on TV,” Olson said. “Every violent crime is not a murder of a police officer. And every crime on the CTA does not involve someone lit on fire. That's a challenge for the public. ... There are different forms of battery and assault.”</p><h3>Federal funding threat</h3><p>When federal transportation officials threatened last December to withhold grant money over safety concerns, the CTA responded by adding more cops and police dogs to patrol the transit system in December.</p><p>Leerhsen said a volunteer unit of officers working overtime transit shifts had increased more than 55%, to at least 120 officers on an average day.</p><p>But the Federal Transit Administration <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/12/19/president-trump-chicago-cta-crime-security-funding-cut-mayor-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" ><u>said that wasn’t enough</u></a>. The agency has now given the CTA until March 19 to propose a revised safety plan or risk losing the $50 million in funding.</p><p>Since then, Leerhsen credited bulked up police patrols on the Red and Blue lines in the past year — and accompanying decreases in reported crimes and customer complaints on those lines. She has also touted a significant increase in transit-related tickets and more overall police visibility on trains and platforms.</p><p>The CTA claimed the increased patrols have contributed to an 8.5% drop in overall crime compared to the same period last year. Meanwhile, rail crime has fallen 10%, and violent crime on the bus system has dropped 23%.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://flo.uri.sh/visualisation/27782644/embed" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" height="575" width="700" style="width:100%;" title="Interactive or visual content"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>The police department said overall arrests on the transit system increased last year, even as the number of officers assigned to the two transit units fell. Staffing numbers for those units reported by the city’s inspector general’s office don’t account for the cops volunteering to work overtime.</p><p>The department said officers work with the CTA “to address public safety concerns as they arise and implement proactive efforts to reduce crime and restore a sense of safety to ridership.”</p><p>That includes upgrading a high-tech command center last year and including officers from its robbery task force to quickly relay information to other cops.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson won’t say how he wants transit security to change.</p><p>Instead, a mayoral spokesman said Johnson takes safety concerns seriously as the city continues to implement a “holistic response” centered on mental health services and homelessness outreach.</p><h3>‘Our job is deterrence’</h3><p>Community activist Tio Hardiman has been leading a group of Violence Interrupters on Red Line trains since 2022 to prevent transit crimes.</p><p>Hardiman said assaults and batteries are usually crimes of opportunity or the result of arguments with no one to mediate.</p><p>He said his volunteers have prevented 15 potential attacks on Red Line trains since November. “Our job is deterrence,” he noted.</p><p>But he said there’s also a need for “a community outreach component on the train,” similar to the approach Mayor Johnson has championed. A security presence can only do so much, Hardiman acknowledged.</p><p>“The reality is that the people who break the law on the trains are not scared of the police," he said.</p><div entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: How Chicago has grappled with crime on the CTA over the last 60 years
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Transit Authority is no stranger to danger.</p><p>Dating back to at least the 1960s, headlines in the Sun-Times spotlighted high-profile crimes on the system and the city government's response.</p><p>Every decade or so, another series of stories reported a rise in crime — and the mayoral administration's attempts to resolve it.</p><p>In 1967, Mayor Richard J. Daley responded to a series of CTA slayings by calling for cops on every train and station.</p><p>That didn't happen.</p><p>But Daley disbanded CTA's security force that patrolled trains and buses and replaced it with Chicago police officers — a change that remains to this day.</p><p></p><p></p><p>Jump to 2026: The federal government is threatening to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/09/19/no-cash-bail-us-transportation-secretary-sean-duffy-federal-funding-cta-crime" target="_blank" >cut millions in funding</a> from the CTA if it doesn't improve its security program after a series of high-profile crimes.</p><p>The CTA says it has increased the number of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/01/22/cta-crime-police-officer-transit-rider-interaction-program" target="_blank" >officers patrolling trains</a>, but the federal government says <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/12/19/president-trump-chicago-cta-crime-security-funding-cut-mayor-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" >it’s not enough.</a></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/02/chicago-cta-transit-crime-assault-battery-leerhsen" >CTA suffers most violent attacks in decades as Trump threatens $50 million funding cut over crime</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><i>Take a trip back through the Sun-Times archives to see how the city has responded to high-profile transit crimes:</i></p><h3>1960s: Daley puts cops on trains</h3><p>"Fear on the CTA" was a front-page banner headline in the Sun-Times' Sept. 5, 1967, issue.</p><p>The first of a three-part series examined the rise of assaults and robberies on the transit system. The reporters concluded that CTA security was understaffed and inadequate.</p><p>"The evidence indicates that the CTA views its passengers as so many parcels to be delivered from one point to another. If they are damaged along the way, a claim card may be filled out," the reporters wrote.</p><p>In response, Richard J. Daley called for Chicago cops to patrol every train and station, day and night. The city wound up deploying around 200 officers, in addition to CTA's security force that patrolled the system.</p><p>That police transit force remained in place, replacing the CTA's. Six months later, the police superintendent claimed the increased patrols had tamped down crime and resulted in 1,700 arrests.</p><p>Crime was still a problem the next year, in 1968, when the Sun-Times reported a string of bus driver robberies. The city responded by assigning 300 officers to patrol buses in a similar crackdown.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-850000" name="image-850000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Mayor Michael Bilandic proposed adding 100 "transit security aides" to the CTA following rising transit crime and the fatal stabbing of a passenger at the Oak Park Austin station." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2fb5c4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2080x1167+0+155/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Feb%2Fad0703914e7389310a4970a1676f%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-06-at-1-54-05-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64a9c9b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2080x1167+0+155/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Feb%2Fad0703914e7389310a4970a1676f%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-06-at-1-54-05-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2fb5c4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2080x1167+0+155/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbf%2Feb%2Fad0703914e7389310a4970a1676f%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-06-at-1-54-05-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mayor Michael Bilandic proposed adding 100 “transit security aides” to the CTA following rising transit crime and the fatal stabbing of a passenger at the Oak Park Austin station.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>NewsBank archives</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>1970s: Transit security aides proposed</h3><p>Transit crime grabbed headlines again in 1978 after the fatal stabbing of 22-year-old Rita L. Hopkinson at Oak Park's Austin Avenue L station. Mayor Michael Bilandic responded by proposing to add 100 "transit security aides" to the CTA.</p><p>A CTA union official blasted the plan as not enough. And there's no report of the aides ever being deployed. After the slaying of a bus driver the same year, Bilandic ordered a review of CTA security to avoid a walkout of CTA personnel.</p><h3>1980s: CTA security force disbanded</h3><p>Mayor Jane Byrne disbanded the CTA police in 1981, accusing the force — which at that point only patrolled CTA garages and rail yards — of not doing their job and allowing drug sales at the properties.</p><p>Later in the decade, crime began rising again and prompted Mayor Harold Washington in 1987 to deploy 100 moonlighting cops to patrol buses.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-credit"><p>NewsBank archive</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>1990s: Conductors cut from trains</h3><p>Facing deep federal funding cuts in 1997, the CTA began to get rid of its conductors on the Red and Blue lines. These were employees who rode trains in addition to the motormen who pilot them.</p><p>The Sun-Times editorial board wrote in March 1998: "[T]he absence of conductors combined with the new crime statistics only strengthen the perception among nervous riders that our transit system is not as safe as it should be."</p><h3>2010s: More cameras, more crime</h3><p>The CTA finished installing 3,600 security cameras at train stations in 2013, but crime increased on the system. The CTA defended its record by saying the chance of a crime at a station remains small.</p><h3><b>2020s: A fiery attack</b></h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/8/5/23289322/cta-crime-dan-beam-subway-l-violent-crimes" target="_blank" >Rising crime</a> against CTA passengers following the COVID-19 pandemic prompts agency head Dorval Carter Jr. to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/8/24/23320509/cta-k9-dog-contract-security-public-transit" target="_blank" >reinstate canine patrols</a> in 2022.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/11/19/terrorism-charges-filed-after-woman-lit-on-fire-on-blue-line-in-loop" target="_blank" >set ablaze on the Blue Line</a> last year, the federal government is threatening to withhold funding if the CTA doesn't improve its safety plan to its liking.</p><p>The CTA said it was increasing the number of CPD officers working overtime on its volunteer transit unit in December. But the Federal Transit Administration said it wasn't good enough and gave the CTA a March 19 deadline to submit a new safety plan — or the FTA would withhold up to $50 million in funding.</p><p>The CTA has said it will submit an updated security plan by the deadline.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="Trump administration threatens $50 million CTA funding cut, says security plan is 'materially deficient'" target="_blank" >Trump administration threatens $50 million CTA funding cut, says security plan is ‘materially deficient’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/09/19/no-cash-bail-us-transportation-secretary-sean-duffy-federal-funding-cta-crime" target="_blank" >Cut CTA crime or lose federal funding, U.S. Transportation chief threatens</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/11/19/terrorism-charges-filed-after-woman-lit-on-fire-on-blue-line-in-loop" target="_blank" >‘Brutal and vicious’ Blue Line attack leads to terrorism charge after woman set on fire</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Jesse Jackson’s lyrical swagger and political ‘juice’ resonates as students learn about his legacy
entries.summary: <p>A few days after the Rev. Jesse Jackson’s death, teacher Erik Young pulled up a quote from the civil rights leader in his classroom at King College Prep High School.</p><p>“I was born in the slum, but the slum wasn’t born in me,” the line on the screen read. Young asked his students to think about what it meant.</p><p>Hands shot up in the air in the North Kenwood classroom. "It means you don’t have to be a product of your environment,” one student said in a quiet but confident voice.</p><p>To Raqeebat Bankole, a junior, it meant if you have a good mindset, “you can succeed in anything,” no matter where you started. That idea resonated with her.</p><p>Like many of her classmates, Raqeebat wasn’t all that familiar with Jackson’s work before he died Feb. 17. But after her class embarked on a crash course on Jackson’s life over several days, she and her classmates came away with a newfound respect for what he accomplished.</p><p>“Now that I’ve learned about him, he’s really impressive,” Raqeebat said.</p><p>Or, as another student put it: “That man was a boss.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d60000" name="image-d60000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/58172b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7769x4360+0+411/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F31%2F3ecb7c3b410eba03164dff25d59e%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-009.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/206ef15/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7769x4360+0+411/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F31%2F3ecb7c3b410eba03164dff25d59e%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-009.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7b3938/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7769x4360+0+411/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F31%2F3ecb7c3b410eba03164dff25d59e%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="11th grader Raqeebat Bankole joins the discussion during a social science lesson on the late Rev. Jesse Jackson at King College Prep on Feb. 19." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7b3938/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7769x4360+0+411/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F31%2F3ecb7c3b410eba03164dff25d59e%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-009.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/50bf730/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7769x4360+0+411/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F31%2F3ecb7c3b410eba03164dff25d59e%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-009.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7b3938/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7769x4360+0+411/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F31%2F3ecb7c3b410eba03164dff25d59e%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Junior Raqeebat Bankole joins the discussion during a social science lesson on the late Rev. Jesse Jackson at King College Prep on Feb. 19.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The lessons happening at King are one example of how a new generation is learning about Jackson and identifying with him in their own ways. Students in Young’s class connected with Jackson’s lyrical turns of phrase, likening his swagger to that of a hip-hop artist, and admired the fearlessness with which he fought for the Black community.</p><p>The students sat facing each other on either side of the screen with the Jackson quotes, forming a sort of semi-circle that allowed the discussion to flow freely. Young, in a white, yellow and green tie-dye King College Prep T-shirt, alternated energetically between standing and sitting. He encouraged all his students to speak up, calling on not only those with raised hands but also those trying to hide in their hoodies.</p><p>Young, a social science teacher, has taught at King for 18 years. He included mentions of Jackson and his work in Chicago in his lessons before, but after Jackson’s death, Young decided it was time for his world studies class to take a deeper dive.</p><p>“Many of the students here really had not heard of him, and it's no fault of their own,” Young said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a20000" name="image-a20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee2f932/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7271x4081+0+385/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fc8%2Fa6dbe2f5452cb8206324b72326bf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-018.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15eb74c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7271x4081+0+385/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fc8%2Fa6dbe2f5452cb8206324b72326bf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-018.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ab5859/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7271x4081+0+385/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fc8%2Fa6dbe2f5452cb8206324b72326bf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-018.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Social science teacher Erik Young leading a lesson the Reverend Jesse Jackson at King College Prep on February 19." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ab5859/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7271x4081+0+385/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fc8%2Fa6dbe2f5452cb8206324b72326bf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-018.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/96d36cf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7271x4081+0+385/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fc8%2Fa6dbe2f5452cb8206324b72326bf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-018.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ab5859/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7271x4081+0+385/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fc8%2Fa6dbe2f5452cb8206324b72326bf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-018.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Social science teacher Erik Young introduced some his students at King College Prep to the Rev. Jesse Jackson and his legacy for the first time during a series of lessons at the Chicago public school shortly after Jackson died on Feb. 17. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>King College Prep is a selective enrollment school on Chicago’s South Side. It’s only a few blocks from the headquarters of Jackson’s Rainbow PUSH Coalition. Jackson frequently spoke at the school, notably after the 2013 murder of Hadiya Pendleton, who was a student there.</p><p>Chicago Public Schools includes material on Jackson and Rainbow PUSH in its vetted Skyline curriculum, which is used by many schools across the district. But officials say they plan to expand those offerings.</p><p>Starting in school year 2027, the district’s social science curriculum will include a new unit titled “Resistance Through Coalition Building,” which the district said will explore Jackson’s legacy.</p><p>“This unit challenges students to define what solidarity looks like, sounds like, and feels like across communities,” a CPS spokesperson wrote in a statement.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson said schools across America should teach about Jackson’s work - but added he hopes those lessons include conversations about the media’s role in how Jackson was viewed.</p><p>“All of our schools across the state of Illinois and this country should highlight the impeccable legacy of Rev. Jackson,” Johnson said. “If we’re going to teach the legacy of Rev. Jackson, we should also be especially critical of how mainstream, corporatized presentation can demonize someone who is, quite frankly, is one of the most dynamic human beings to ever walk the planet.”</p><p>Junior Ke’miah Richardson said she admired Jackson’s bravery after reading about the setbacks he faced, such as his two unsuccessful presidential runs.</p><p>“I feel like that takes a lot of courage, especially if you didn’t get it the first time,” Ke’miah said. “That stuck out to me.”</p><p>Young’s class also discussed Jackson’s diplomatic efforts to free hostages abroad. Several students were amazed that Jackson influenced world affairs despite not holding an official political office. They said he had “juice” — slang for power.</p><p>Damiyah Eberhart, another student, was drawn to Jackson’s poetic language because it was a reminder of the musical history that connects Black communities, like hip-hop.</p><p>“It was kind of his way of showing his love for his own community in his speeches,” Damiyah said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c60000" name="image-c60000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/948fa69/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F80%2Fd80c8149486ca111ce0245a761cf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8409fe4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F80%2Fd80c8149486ca111ce0245a761cf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-012.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a674bce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F80%2Fd80c8149486ca111ce0245a761cf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Teacher Erik Young shows the iconic photo of the Rev. Jesse Jackson with Dr. Martin Luther King in Memphis soon before King was assassinated during a lesson about about the Rev. Jackson at King College Prep." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a674bce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F80%2Fd80c8149486ca111ce0245a761cf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2e65a68/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F80%2Fd80c8149486ca111ce0245a761cf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-012.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a674bce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F80%2Fd80c8149486ca111ce0245a761cf%2Fjacksonlesson-260223-012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Teacher Erik Young posted images, including this iconic photo of the Rev. Jesse Jackson with Dr. Martin Luther King in Memphis soon before King was assassinated, during a recent world studies class about Rev. Jackson at King College Prep. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Though Young didn’t grow up in Chicago, he has his own memories of Jackson. He remembers watching Jackson on TV as a child in Philadelphia and being awestruck by Jackson’s command of a crowd.</p><p>“I was always enthralled and inspired by that,” Young said.</p><p>Young hopes that by learning of Jackson’s trajectory, today’s high schoolers will understand “that they can break out and create their own path.”</p><p>That was true for Damiyah, who left feeling motivated after the lessons in Young’s class.</p><p>“It will make me want to do more, especially as a Black youth,” Damiyah said. “Getting out there kind of protesting, spreading my name across my community, anything to get my community the glory that it deserves.”</p><p><i>Contributing: Mariah Woelfel</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5d0000" name="module-5d0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><ul id="rte-2f5a1752-1354-11f1-9468-4ffaee5f793a"><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/18/friends-and-family-say-chicago-embraced-and-nurtured-civil-rights-icon-jesse-jackson-from-the-start" >Friends and family say Chicago embraced and nurtured civil rights icon Jesse Jackson from the start</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-mayors-a-fraught-and-contentious-relationship" >Rev. Jesse Jackson and Chicago mayors — a tense, fraught relationship</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/21/how-jesse-jackson-shaped-chicago-education-from-protesting-school-segregation-to-reopening-dyett-hs" >How Jesse Jackson shaped Chicago education, from protesting school segregation to reopening Dyett HS</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/in-the-loop-with-sasha-ann-simons/2026/02/19/how-the-late-rev-jesse-jackson-was-a-pioneer-of-the-progressive-movement-and-lgbtq-ally" >Listen: How the late Rev. Jesse Jackson was a pioneer of the progressive movement and LGBTQ+ ally</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2026/02/20/jesse-jackson-economic-employment-justice-boycott-civil-rights" >For Jesse Jackson, economic justice and civil rights were intertwined</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/18/lights-camera-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-pop-culture-presence" >Lights, camera, Jackson: Civil Rights leader also became a pop culture presence</a></li></ul></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Man sitting in vehicle fatally shot in Austin
entries.summary: <p>A man sitting in a vehicle was fatally shot Monday morning in Austin on the West Side. </p><p>The unidentified man, 45, was in the 5400 block of West Jackson Boulevard around 6:50 a.m. when at least one person approached and opened fire, Chicago police said. </p><p>He was struck in the chest and abdomen and was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>Area 4 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Man killed, 15-year-old boy among 3 wounded in weekend drive-by shootings citywide
entries.summary: <p>A man was killed and a 15-year-old boy was among three others wounded in a string of drive by shootings that occurred within three hours citywide Sunday night. </p><p>The string of shootings started when Carlos Navarro Perez, 45, was standing outside in 3900 block of West 62nd Street in West Lawn around 8:30 p.m. and was shot in the head, Chicago police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. Perez, of the same block, was pronounced dead at 9:04 p.m. </p><p>Around 10:05 p.m., a 15-year-old boy was in the 6600 block of South Rhodes Avenue in Woodlawn when he was struck in the abdomen and arm by gunfire, police said. He was taken to Comer Children’s Hospital where he was in fair condition, police said. </p><p>About an hour later, two men were in a moving vehicle in the 5600 block of West Wrightwood Avenue in Belmont Cragin when they were struck by gunfire, officials said.<br>An 18-year-old man was shot in the thigh and the second man, 21, was shot in the elbow, police said. They were taken to St. Mary's Hospital in good condition. </p><p>In each of the incidents, preliminary information suggests the shooter was inside a black vehicle at the time of the attacks. Police say the vehicles haven’t been confirmed to be linked to the shootings. </p><p>Area detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Violent CTA attacks surge
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Violent attacks on the CTA have surged to the most in decades, as President Donald Trump threatens a $50 million funding cut over crime.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Chicagoans with ties to Iran share their worries, a judge orders 32 ICE detainees released and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 K<b>eeping score</b>:<a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-j/" target="_blank" > The Bulls beat the Bucks</a>, 120-97; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-t/" target="_blank" >the Blackhawks bested the Mammoth</a>, 4-0.</p><p>🗓️ <b>Just to note</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-i/" target="_blank" >Happy Pulaski Day</a> to all who celebrate! class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-h/" target="_blank" >CTA suffers most violent attacks in decades as Trump threatens $50M cut over crime</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-k/" target="_blank" >David Struett</a></h5><p><b>Wrong direction</b>: Aggravated assaults and batteries on the Chicago Transit Authority reached a 24-year high in 2025 as the Trump administration threatened to cut millions of dollars in funding after a series of jarring attacks. The upward trend has continued into 2026, with those crimes climbing 33% over the same period last year, according to city data dating back to 2001.</p><p><b>By the numbers</b>: Attacks on the CTA rose last year even as the same crimes fell 15% citywide between 2024 and 2025 — the largest drop in a decade. The police department recorded 469 aggravated assaults and batteries on the CTA last year, more than the 441 reported in 2024, city data shows. A new record for those crimes has been set every year since 2021.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: It’s bad news for the CTA and its interim leader, Nora Leerhsen, who’s trying to highlight positive crime trends on the transit system and secure $50 million in grant funding from the federal government. Unsatisfied with the authority’s decision in December to boost the number of officers patrolling trains and buses, the Federal Transit Administration has <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-u/" target="_blank" >threatened to withhold the money</a> if the CTA doesn’t enact an acceptable safety plan by mid-March.</p><p><b>Troubling trend</b>: The CTA has touted an overall drop in crime, due in part to falling reports of robberies and thefts. But the record number of felony assaults and batteries underscores the work still needed to improve the authority’s image and appease the feds.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>What do you think would be an effective way to address violent crime on the CTA? Explain.</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>NATION AND WORLD ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-070000" name="image-070000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c2dc159/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F2d%2Fe5973d0c4d3c913f3d95ac3356b4%2Fprotest-26301-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8079297/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F2d%2Fe5973d0c4d3c913f3d95ac3356b4%2Fprotest-26301-03.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/24efc92/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F2d%2Fe5973d0c4d3c913f3d95ac3356b4%2Fprotest-26301-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Protesters call for an end to the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran during a demonstration in Federal Plaza in the Loop, Saturday, Feb. 28, 2026. " class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-b/" target="_blank" >Chicagoans with ties to Iran and the Middle East worry as bombs fall</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-n/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-p/" target="_blank" >Kaitlin Washburn</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-x/" target="_blank" >Kade Heather</a></h5><p><b>Anxious waiting</b>: Talla Mountjoy has been sitting by her phone on a charger, waiting for calls from family in <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-m/" target="_blank" >Iran since the U.S. and Israel attacked</a>. Bombs razed areas around the neighborhood in Tehran where her grandmother and other loved ones live, but so far they have been safe, said Mountjoy, a senior director at the University of Chicago’s Forum for Free Inquiry and Expression. "I just want to be able to connect with my family in real time again," she said, noting communications blackouts pose difficulties.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: U.S. and Israeli forces launched a major attack on the country early Saturday, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-c/" target="_blank" >killing its supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei</a>, and other high-ranking officials, the Associated Press reported. At least 200 people were killed on the first day of fighting, including 115 reported dead when a girls’ school was struck — <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-q/" target="_blank" >and at least four U.S. service members</a>. Retaliatory attacks from Iran and Iranian-backed militias <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-a/" target="_blank" >have hit Israel and other Middle Eastern nations</a>.</p><p><b>Chicago protests</b>: Dozens of antiwar activists <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-z/" target="_blank" >descended Saturday evening onto Federal Plaza in the Loop </a>to denounce the U.S.-Israel attack. Earlier in the day, about 100 demonstrators in Chicago who supported the strikes rallied in Daley Plaza, flying American flags and the prerevolution Iranian flag. At Saturday evening’s protest, Iranian American and refugee rights advocate Ali Tarokh said, "The message is simple: Stop this war." </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-v/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>REV. Jesse Jackson at King College Prep High School in Kenwood. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-e/" target="_blank" >Students get crash course of life of Jesse Jackson and are wowed by legacy</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-s/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Inside the classroom</b>: Many students at Kenwood's King College Prep High School say they aren't all that familiar with the work of the late Rev. Jesse Jackson. But after one class embarked on a crash course on Jackson’s life over several days, students said they came away with a newfound respect for what he accomplished. "Now that I’ve learned about him, he’s really impressive," one student said. "That man was a boss," another said.</p><p><b>New generation</b>: The lessons happening at King are one example of how a new generation is learning about Jackson and identifying with him in their own ways. Students at King connected with Jackson’s lyrical turns of phrase, likening his swagger to that of a hip-hop artist, and admired the fearlessness with which he fought for the Black community.</p><p><b>Teacher's take</b>: Erik Young, a social science teacher, has taught at King for 18 years. He included mentions of Jackson and his work in Chicago in his lessons before, but after Jackson’s death, Young decided it was time for his world studies class to take a deeper dive. "Many of the students here really had not heard of him, and it’s no fault of their own," he said.</p><p><b>More on Jackson</b>: <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-2331f5e5-1644-11f1-ab55-9f23f492b098"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-g/" target="_blank" >Young mourners reflect on Rev. Melissa Bean</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mendy Kong/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-jh/" target="_blank" >Pro-Israel group’s donors and affiliates pour $13.7M into Chicago-area primaries</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-jk/" target="_blank" >Mariah Woelfel</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-ju/" target="_blank" >Mawa Iqbal </a>and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-tl/" target="_blank" >Chip Mitchell</a></h5><p><b>Spending big</b>: Donors with the American Israel Public Affairs Committee and its affiliates have spent $13.7 million on the group’s preferred candidates in four wide-open Chicago-area congressional primaries, a WBEZ analysis of federal campaign disclosures found.</p><p><b>Outstanding issue</b>: Several Democratic candidates in these races reportedly met with AIPAC and learned the group had drawn a hard line on one thing: Unconditional U.S. military aid to Israel. The topic has become a flashpoint for candidates and voters as more Democrats call for improving human rights in Gaza.</p><p><b>New survey</b>: WBEZ surveyed the 46 candidates in those March 17 primaries about whether there should be strict U.S. conditions on military aid to Israel, whether that country has committed genocide in Gaza, and whether AIPAC funding should be off-limits in these races. Most respondents said "yes" to all the questions. Four AIPAC-aligned candidates, all Democrats, would not answer "yes" or "no": Cook County Commissioner Donna Miller in the 2nd District; Chicago City Treasurer Melissa Conyears-Ervin in the 7th; former U.S. Rep. Melissa Bean in the 8th; and state Sen. Laura Fine in the 9th.</p><p><b>Early voting widens</b>: More early voting sites have opened, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-tr/" target="_blank" >including 50 ward voting sites in the city and 55 sites in suburban Cook County</a>. Prepare<a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-ty/" target="_blank" > </a>for the March 17 primary with <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-tj/" target="_blank" >our voter guide</a> and get a <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-tt/" target="_blank" >preview of your ballot</a>.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-ti/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX ⚾🏀</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-23329220-1644-11f1-ab55-9f23f492b098"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-td/" target="_blank" ><b>Sox’ strong start</b></a>: The White Sox’ young catchers are raising expectations heading into their second seasons.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-th/" target="_blank" ><b>DePaul’s getting it done</b></a>: After completing a season sweep of Marquette, the Blue Demons have their most Big East men's basketball victories in 19 years and three Big East road wins in a row for the first time ever.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-tk/" target="_blank" ><b>Ramblers’ rough patch</b></a>: Loyola men's basketball coach Drew Valentine is addressing the team's "bizarre" 7-22 season.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-tu/" target="_blank" ><b>Boys basketball</b></a>: Lockport, Simeon, St. Laurence and Hersey return after winning regionals, and Brother Rice debuts in Michael O'Brien's Super 25 high school basketball rankings for March 1.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-il/" target="_blank" ><b>Girls basketball</b></a>: We’ve arrived at the final week of the season.
