Each year, participants raise money for Special Olympics Illinois, and this year’s group has garnered more than $1.5 million in donations, falling short of the $2 million goal and down from last year’s $1.7 million, according to the event’s website.</p><p>Swimmers — some in bathing suits, others in full costume — ran into the lake and dunked, shrieked and gasped from the cold. The lake temperature was 35 degrees as they jumped in. Crayons, Santa Claus and hot dogs were among the costumes participants donned.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-id/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated</b> by: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjdija-l-ik/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Blackhawks' Andre Burakovsky thankful for Jeff Blashill's trust as he searches for confidence
entries.summary: <p>SALT LAKE CITY — Andre Burakovsky has become a lightning rod for criticism from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> fans, who cannot understand why he's cemented in a first-line spot despite a lengthy slump.</p><p>Just because Hawks coach Jeff Blashill believes in Burakovsky, though, doesn't mean Burakovsky wholeheartedly believes in himself right now.</p><p>This rough stretch — zero goals and one assist in his last 16 games — has weighed on the veteran forward's conscience.</p><p>"Things have not been coming easy," Burakovsky said Sunday. "I've experienced this before in my career, where maybe you start doubting yourself a little bit and confidence is going down and it becomes harder to play. I just worked on myself during the break and tried to build my confidence and get back to just [being] me."</p><p>He was fine in the Hawks' 4-0 blowout <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/01/blackhawks-mammoth-defensive-systems-arvid-soderblom-alex-vlasic" target="_blank" >win over the Mammoth</a> and has been slightly better overall in three games since the Olympic break, at least reducing his frequency of turnovers and mistakes. But the scoresheet remains elusive.</p><p>It's a tricky situation for Blashill to manage. On one hand, Burakovsky undoubtedly hasn't produced the way a top-line forward should recently.</p><p>On the other hand, Blashill has seen many great players encounter confidence crises in the past, and he has found that keeping them in their usual role gives them the best chance to break out of it.</p><p>"'Burky' and I had a good meeting right before the break," Blashill said last week. "You can tell he was in a little bit of a struggle. It was interesting for me because...in 10-game segments, the segment that we ended with, his scoring-chance plus-minus wasn’t great. But the prior segments were all really good.</p><p>"So [he] was only 10 games away from playing really good hockey. That was a reminder, and I told him, 'This is a reminder for me as much as it is for you.' Because when he’s struggling, generally the coach is struggling with him."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Burakovsky turns it over and Coyle finishes for his second of the game <a href="https://t.co/iyzmv89Rjw">pic.twitter.com/iyzmv89Rjw</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2017427133891526660?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">January 31, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Another factor is the fact the Hawks don't have an abundance of forwards — yet, that is — that they believe possess the necessary skills to play with top-line center Connor Bedard.</p><p>"When [Bedard] gives the puck to you, you've got to have enough give-and-go in your game to make sure you get it back to him at the right spots, [and] you've got to be able to transport it at times," Blashill said Sunday, adding that he plans to give Burakovsky "another little bit of runway" before potentially making any changes.</p><p>Burakovsky does sincerely appreciate Blashill's continued investment in him.</p><p>"You feel like you have the coach's trust, and that helps you with confidence, too," he said. "But I also want to play better for him because he shows a lot of faith in me."</p><p>During the Olympic break, Burakovsky opted for a full mental refresh — a family vacation to Mexico — instead of working on random skills at a random rink somewhere.</p><p>"[I was] trying to get away from hockey over the break, not think about things," he said. "The more you think about it, the worse it's going to get."</p><p>With 30 points in 54 games, he's still averaging more points per game this season (0.56) than he did in his last two seasons with the Kraken. And with another year left on his contract, that's not a cause of stress.</p><p>He has reflected examples of adversity from his first 11 NHL seasons to remind himself this is only temporary.</p><p>"I always overcame it," he said. "I always do, eventually. But you want it to be maybe one or two games and then jump back. Now, it's been a little longer for me. I'm still working on it.</p><p>"I've been feeling a little better...[although there are] still some puck touches that surprise me. I don't know why, but it does sometimes. I'm just going to clean that up and play with confidence."</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Gunman shoots at Chicago cops leading to chase, crash in Auburn Gresham
entries.summary: <p>A gunman allegedly fired shots at Chicago police officers Monday morning, sparking a chase that ended in a crash and at least one arrest in the Auburn Gresham neighborhood on the city's South Side. </p><p>Officers were inside two marked squad cars just after 6 a.m. in the 7500 block of South Peoria Street when the gunman, who was on foot nearby, began shooting at the officers, police said. </p><p>No one was shot and officers didn’t return fire, officials said. </p><p>The alleged shooter then fled into a black SUV and officers chased him until he crashed into two parked vehicles in the 7600 block of South Union Avenue, police said. </p><p>The alleged shooter tried running away but was arrested and taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center for observation, officials said. </p><p>Two officers were taken to area hospitals for observation and have since been released, authorities said. </p><p>A weapon was recovered at the scene and Area 1 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Más sitios de votación temprana abren el lunes en Chicago y suburbios
entries.summary: <p>Más sitios de votación anticipada abren el lunes en Chicago y los suburbios del Condado de Cook.</p><p>Hoy abren los <a class="Link" href="https://chicagoelections.gov/voting/early-voting" target="_blank" >50 sitios de votación anticipada en los distritos</a> de Chicago, así como <a class="Link" href="https://www.cookcountyclerkil.gov/elections/ways-to-vote/early-voting/early-voting-locations" target="_blank" >55 sitios en los suburbios del Condado de Cook</a>. Los votantes de Chicago ya han podido votar temprano antes del Día de las Elecciones del 17 de marzo en la oficina de la Junta de Elecciones de Chicago en 69 W. Washington St. y en el supersitio en el centro en 137 S. State St.</p><p>También hoy lunes abren más sitios de votación anticipada en los condados aledaños. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/how-to-vote-illinois-primary" target="_blank" >Encuentra una lista completa aquí</a>.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-4e0000" name="module-4e0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Los votantes que deseen votar por correo aún pueden inscribirse para recibir una boleta por correo. La fecha límite para solicitarla es a las 5 p.m. el 12 de marzo.</p><p>Hasta la noche del sábado, se han emitido 3,165 boletas de voto anticipado en Chicago y se han recibido 24,612 boletas de voto por correo, según la Junta de Elecciones de Chicago.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: What we know about Rev. Jesse Jackson's death and plans to honor his life
entries.summary: <p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" target="_blank" >“died peacefully”</a> Feb. 17, his family said during a news conference last week. He was 84. </p><p>Jackson is a Chicago icon known for his work as a civil rights leader. "All are welcome" for services scheduled this week, according to his family.</p><h3>When are services?</h3><p><b></b><b></b></p><p></p><p>On <b>Monday</b>, a full day of recognition will take place at and near the South Carolina state capitol, according to communications from Rainbow PUSH. </p><p>Jackson will lie in repose at the Statehouse in Columbia before private, public and church services take place throughout the day. A formal service will take place at 10 a.m. at 1100 Gervais St., Columbia, S.C., 29208. Later that afternoon, a tribute service will be held at Brookland Baptist Church from 4 p.m. to 6 p.m at 1066 Sunset Blvd. Pastor Charles B. Jackson Sr. will officiate. </p><p><b>A formal service will no longer take place in Washington, D.C., according to the latest update on </b><a class="Link" href="JesseJacksonLegacy.com" target="_blank" ><b>JesseJacksonLegacy.com</b></a><b>.</b> </p><p>Omega Psi Phi Fraternity Inc., a Divine Nine fraternity that Jackson belonged to, will hold a celebration of life service at 5 p.m. <b>Thursday</b> at Rainbow PUSH. Grand officers, district officers and various members of the fraternity will be in attendance, said a district representative. </p><p>A "People's Celebration" in Chicago will be held at 10 a.m. <b>Friday</b>, at House of Hope, 752 E. 114th St. Doors open at 9 a.m. Additional parking will be available at nearby businesses. Shuttles will then transport people to the venue.</p><p>Janet Pickett, the general manager at House of Hope, said they are planning for more than 9,000 people to attend. (The venue seats 9,600.) There is no word yet on who will be leading the services, she said. Ushers and personnel for the event will be staffed by House of Hope and Rainbow PUSH. </p><p>Private homegoing services are scheduled for 10 a.m. <b>Saturday</b>, at Rainbow PUSH. Doors open at 8 a.m., with limited capacity. </p><p>Relatives of Jackson said that "all are welcome," regardless of political party.</p><p>"Do not bring your politics, out of respect to Rev. Jesse Jackson and the life that he lived, to these homegoing services. They come respectful and come to say thank you," Jesse Jackson Jr. said during a media event in front of the Jackson family home. "But these homegoing services are welcome to all — Democrat, Republican, liberal and conservative, right wing, left wing, because his life is broad enough to cover the full spectrum of what it means to be an American."</p><p>The family will livestream all services at JesseJacksonLegacy.com. </p><h3>How have Chicagoans paid their respects?</h3><p>Thousands gathered at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/26/rev-jesse-jackson-lying-in-repose-rainbow-push-chicago" >Rainbow PUSH headquarter last week</a> to pay their respects to Jackson. The family procession arrived after 8 a.m. As the morning wore on, hundreds were lined up to show their gratitude for Jackson and be a part of a historical moment. </p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson and the Rev. Al Sharpton were in attendance Thursday to pay their respects.</p><p>"We spent a lifetime together," said Sharpton outside the Rainbow PUSH headquarters. "He raised a generation of us, and he never gave up, even when he was sick." </p><p>Crowds included <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/27/jesse-jacksons-death-inspires-young-people-more-present-community" >several Black youth who were inspired by Jackson</a> to be more present in their community. </p><p>Among them was Nadiyah Spikener, 24, who said Jackson's death could be a catalyst for young people to learn more about their history. </p><p>"This may be a moment to realize that maybe we need to do more for ourselves, and do more for our community like he has, and just start somewhere," she said. </p><h3>What hotels are recommended for those traveling to Chicago for the events?</h3><p>Those traveling to Chicago to attend services are encouraged to stay at the Hyatt Regency McCormick Place Chicago at 2233 S. Martin Luther King Drive, according to the family's website. Rooms at the hotel have been reserved within a hotel block, with limited availability. </p><h3>Where can people park?</h3><p>For those hoping to attend the Jackson lying in state events, street parking is available at Rainbow PUSH on South Drexel Boulevard, East 49th Street, East Hyde Park Boulevard and East 49th Street. </p><p>For the People's Celebration, parking will be available at House of Hope's parking lot on 113th Street. </p><h3>What security measures will be in place?</h3><p>At each event, security checks and magnetometers will be in place. The family's website asks that individuals limit items brought to smooth the process. Weapons, signs and any items that could disrupt services are prohibited.</p><p>For more information on the scheduled services visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.jessejacksonlegacy.com/" target="_blank" >jessejacksonlegacy.com</a>. </p><h3>Where will the Rev. Jesse Jackson be buried?</h3><p>Jackson will be buried at <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/curious-city/2025/06/04/prejudice-follows-us-to-the-grave-the-segregated-past-of-chicago-cemeteries#:~:text=Mayor%20Harold%20Washington%20is%20buried,bring%20it%20to%20an%20end." target="_blank" >Oak Woods Cemetery</a>, the largest cemetery on the South Side, in Greater Grand Crossing. Former Chicago Mayor Harold Washington, journalist and civil rights activist Ida B. Wells and Olympic athlete Jesse Owens are among those buried there.</p><h3>How did he die?</h3><p>While his family has not given an official cause of death nor said where he died, Jackson had been declining in health for a decade before his death. In 2015,<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/11/17/18375473/rev-jesse-jackson-announces-parkinson-s-diagnosis" > Jackson was initially diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease</a>, but he revealed last April that he actually had progressive supranuclear palsy.</p><h3>How is the political world reacting to his death?</h3><p>Political figures from across the country have weighed in to pay their respects and honor his legacy. </p><p>In a joint statement, former President Barack Obama and former first lady Michelle Obama wrote: "Michelle and I will always be grateful for Jesse's lifetime of service, and the friendship our families share. We stood on his shoulders. We send our deepest condolences to the Jackson family and everyone in Chicago and beyond who knew and loved him." </p><p>President Donald Trump also extended his sympathies to Jackson's family in a statement posted to Truth Social last week. </p><p>"I knew him well, long before becoming President," he said. "He was very gregarious — Someone who truly loved people! Despite the fact that I am falsely and consistently called a Racist by the Scoundrels and Lunatics on the Radical Left, Democrats ALL, it was always my pleasure to help Jesse along the way."</p><p>Chicago leaders, including Mayor Brandon Johnson and Gov. JB Pritzker, also shared their condolences. </p><p>"In honor of Rev. Jackson's service, and as our city mourns, the Chicago flag will lower to half-staff," Johnson wrote in a statement. </p><p>Pritzker also ordered all flags across the state to fly at half-staff to honor him in a statement posted on social media.</p><p>"He broke down barriers, inspired generations, and kept hope alive. Our state, nation, and world are better due to his years of service," Pritzker said.</p><h3>How are Chicagoans reacting to his death?</h3><p>Across the city, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/17/jesse-jackson-chicago-remembers" >Chicago residents mourned Jackson’s death</a> and urged that his legacy continue. </p><p>Martin Travis, a congregant of Apostolic Church of God in Woodlawn, said in his youth, his parents brought him to demonstrations organized by Operation Breadbasket, a movement Jackson led. </p><p>Now, 64, Travis remembers Jackson as "instrumental in fighting for justice and freedom for all people."</p><p>Chloe Gould, 41, a Bronzeville resident, said that his death hit "like you lost a family member." </p><p>In the wake of his death, she said she worries about the future of civil rights activism, as other activists and leaders have not stepped in to fill his shoes. </p><p>"Who is gonna be the civil rights activist now?" she asked. </p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson

entries.title: Man, 19, fatally shot at gender reveal party in Auburn Gresham: police
entries.summary: <p>A celebration that should have been joyful ended tragically as someone in a passing car opened fire, gunning down a 19-year-old man who was attending a gender reveal party Saturday in Auburn Gresham on the South Side, according to Chicago police.</p><p>Michael Rounds Jr. was inside a home about 5 p.m. in the 7700 block of South Racine Avenue when someone in a passing car fired shots, sending bullets shattering through a window and hitting him in the head, police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. Rounds was pronounced dead at the scene. </p><p>Rounds' mother, who was distraught when reached by a Sun-Times reporter, did not immediately share more information. </p><p>According to police reports, Rounds Jr.'s sister told responding officers that her family was celebrating a gender reveal party that was winding down when a blue car pulled up and someone asked "What are you doing on my block?" </p><p>Moments later, the same car returned and fired at the building, leaving Rounds Jr. fatally shot, the report states. </p><p>About 20 .45-caliber and 9-millimeter cartridges were recovered from the scene, police said. It was initially believed there was a second shooting victim but it was later determined Rounds Jr.'s sister had injured her foot on broken glass. </p><p>No one was in custody in the attack of Rounds, who was one of at least <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/02/at-least-6-killed-in-weekend-gun-violence-citywide" target="_blank" >six homicide victims over the weekend. </a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: No red flag for Bulls' Matas Buzelis as forward is starting to surge
entries.summary: <p>It was a slight red flag right when it came out of the mouth of Matas Buzelis last week.</p><p>“I’m just trying to play the game the right way,” the Bulls forward insisted as a nightmarish 0-for-11 winless streak in February was coming to an end.</p><p>The right thing to say, especially for a 21-year-old still trying to find his way, but also all-too familiar babble that struggling Bulls players have said before, only to continue to underwhelm. It’s been a favorite credo of Patrick Williams when he’s asked about playing more aggressively, and even Coby White used to say it in his younger days before he took a jump in output.</p><p>So when Buzelis went to the cliché bag and pulled that one out, yeah, it was a moment to pause. At least until it wasn’t.</p><p>Buzelis and coach Billy Donovan had a heart-to-heart talk the night of Feb. 23 at the Advocate Center, and the message was clear: There are good shots and bad shots – take more good shots, and keep trying to do what it takes to impact winning.</p><p>Message heard.</p><p>In the three games since Buzelis and Donovan had the sit-down, all the second-year player has done is average 24 points per game, to go along with 21 total rebounds and nine assists, while shooting 52% from the field and 42.9% from three-point range. He’s only taken three real mid-range shots, making two – another part of his game Donovan spoke to him about – and he is trying to play with more physicality on both ends, evident by getting to the free throw line 11 times in the last two games.</p><p>In extending his streak of 20-plus points to a career-long three games, Buzelis joined Elton Brand, Luol Deng, and Lauri Markkanen as the only forwards in Bulls history to record such a streak at the age of 21 or younger.</p><p>Has it been the seismic jump that the Buzelis hype train was riding at the start of the season? No, but the top box that should be checked is the Bulls had a drain of talent at the trade deadline, and their young player has stepped up when called upon and needed.</p><p>“Honestly, I’m just trying to remain focused on one goal and that’s winning,” Buzelis said of his mindset since the roster was flipped. “It’s obviously going to be hard. You’re not just going to come in with seven new guys.</p><p>“It doesn’t matter how hard it’s going to be because we’re all in this together, we’re all on the same page. With the scoring and stuff, I’m just trying not to press and play the game the right way all the time. It will come.”</p><p>It has the last week and now it needs to stay.</p><p>Then there’s another layer to Buzelis that is picking up steam. Yes, he’s been a solid rim protector as long as he’s not tied up with a physical big, but he’s also allowed the fewest points per possession when opposing players go isolation on him.</p><p>Buzelis was 0.63 points allowed per possession, while Cooper Flagg and Kyshawn George were right behind him at 0.65. Again, statistics can be twisted a lot of different ways, especially because James Harden is also on this list (tied for sixth) and isn’t scaring anyone with his overall defense, but Buzelis currently has a better defensive efficiency rating than both Williams and Isaac Okoro – each considered defensive-minded wings.</p><p>These are all good things for Buzelis emerging as a two-way player and a franchise foundation piece, especially paired with his mindset.</p><p>“One thing I try to build is a short memory for myself and focusing on the next game,” Buzelis said. “As soon as I get out of the shower, you are onto the next game.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Yorkville wins its first regional title in 21 years
entries.summary: <p>When the IHSA released the sectional seeds and regional matchups a couple of weeks ago, Yorkville was enjoying a team dinner.</p><p>After a few minutes of dissecting things, Yorkville coach John Holakovsky saw his team brimming with some confidence.</p><p>“We saw that bracket during our team dinner and our kids saw a path,” Holakovsky said of the regional matchups. “They saw it and were fired up.”</p><p>What the Foxes initially saw was a regional bracket that avoided having to play what Holakovsky referred to as the sectional’s “Big Three” of Benet, Neuqua Valley and Bolingbrook.</p><p>The sixth-year coach was also intrigued about not having to play other conference foes like Oswego, Oswego East and Plainfield East in the regional.</p><p>“I was excited to play new teams, to be honest,” Holakovsky said. “Super excited to play schools we don’t have any connections to. We went in with a lot of confidence.”</p><p>Holakovsky and the Foxes believed they were “much better” than a No. 13 seed. And they certainly were, both in what they had shown in the regular season and by how they proved it this past week.</p><p>With talented sophomore Joey Jakstys leading the way, the Foxes upset No. 4 seed Naperville North in the regional semifinals. The 6-7 Jakstys knocked down a trio of three-pointers en route to 21 a game-high points and provided a game-changing moment late in the game. Jakstys drove the lane, dunked over a defender, drew a foul and completed the three-point play.</p><p>While promising talent like Jakstys, one of the better young prospects in the state, and fellow sophomore Braydon Porter, who leads the team in scoring, are recognizable names, it was Gabe Sanders and Alonn Flint who led the way in the championship game win over fifth-seeded Downers Grove South.</p><p>Sanders, the senior guard who led the team in scoring a year ago, scored 18 points, while the little-known Flint added 14.</p><p>After playing very little off the bench in the win over Naperville North, Holakovsky told Flint he would play a bigger role in what was a better matchup against DGS. Flint, who averages three points a game, responded.</p><p>As the final seconds ticked down in the convincing 57-40 title game win, Holakovsky said he looked down and saw Yorkville athletic director Luke Engelhardt in front of the student section with an ear-to-ear smile on his face. It was Engelhardt who hired Holakovsky back in 2020 and who was also one of the star players on Yorkville’s last regional championship team in 2005.</p><p>“To see him with that big smile … it was pretty cool and satisfying,” Holakovsky said.</p><p>The Foxes, who improved to 16-12 on the season, claimed their first regional championship in 21 years as the lowest seeded team to advance to a sectional since West Aurora did so as a No. 14 seed in 2021.</p><p>“This is a huge win for the program,” Holakovsky said. “We are trying to grow basketball in our community. This allows those kids who were in the gym for the regional to dream. Having success builds momentum.”</p><p>Now it’s back to reality for the upstart Foxes. The regional title game reward? It’s a sectional semifinal date with Benet, the defending state champs and the No. 1 ranked team in the state.</p><p>“We know what and who Benet is,” Holakovsky said. “I have so much respect for Gene [Heidkamp] and how he does it. I know their staff very well and have a ton of respect for them. We know we have our hands full. But we’re playing with house money now and playing with confidence.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Regional rewind: Breaking down Friday's IHSA state tournament action
entries.summary: <p>By and large, the Cinderella story in IHSA state tournament play is more rare than you think.</p><p>We all get wrapped up in what we believe the Original March Madness here in Illinois provides — an unpredictable nature. But it’s simply not very often low-seeded teams advance beyond the regional.</p><p>The Class 4A state tournament hasn’t had a double-digit seed advance to the sectional since 2022. This year there are two: Yorkville and Simeon.</p><p>Yorkville and Simeon are the two lowest 4A seeds remaining as No. 13 and No. 11 seeds, respectively. How rare is it for a Class 4A double-seeded team to advance beyond the regional?</p><p>In the last 15 completed regionals since 2011 (minus the Covid season), there have been just 11 double-seeded teams (seeded 10th or lower) to win regional championships.</p><p>And since 2011, only five teams seeded 13 or lower have won a regional — Yorkville this season as a No. 13, West Aurora as a No. 14 seed in 2021, DuSable as a 13-seed in 2013, Thornton as a No. 12 in 2012 and Schaumburg, a No. 15 seed in 2011, who went on perhaps the biggest 4A Cinderella run in recent memory.</p><p>Schaumburg, seeded 15th in the sectional, stunned No. 2 seed Evanston in the regional semifinals before taking out seventh-seeded New Trier in the regional championship. Then the Saxons beat another double-seeded team, Niles West, in the sectional semifinal and nearly upset Niles North in the sectional championship, falling 53-52.</p><h3>Simeon as Cinderella?</h3><p>You almost feel like an idiot — or at least foolish — putting Simeon and Cinderella in the same sentence unless it’s someone beating Simeon. After all, it’s a program with seven state championships and a dozen top four finishes. </p><p>However, this is a Simeon team that left the Pontiac Holiday Tournament and proceeded to go 1-3 over the holidays at Pekin. The Wolverines lacked a single marquee win on the season. Now this invigorated team will get a shot at the No. 2 seed, St. Ignatius, in the sectional semifinal.</p><h3>A Central Illinois name to know</h3><p>It’s still early as he’s only a sophomore and the attention is coming, but if Amarion Smith-Holley played his basketball in the Chicago area he would be a household name in high school basketball.</p><p>The Peoria Richwoods point guard dazzled in the Minooka Regional final, teasing Rock Island all night long with a pretty spectacular 31 points. The points came in all shapes and sizes as he led the Knights to an intense and very testy 72-66 win.</p><h3>Catholic League success</h3><p>The Chicago Catholic League was without question the strongest conference in the state during the regular season. With league teams being spread out geographically and into multiple classes, the volume of opportunities for postseason success is abundant in comparison to other leagues.</p><p>But the Catholic League didn’t disappoint and made the most of those opportunities.</p><p>Of the nine teams in the Catholic League Blue, seven won regional championships Friday night, including one of the two losses being one league team knocking the other out (St. Laurence over Mount Carmel).</p><p>Add in three regional titles from the Catholic League White — Leo and Providence both pulled out 3A wins and De Sales was a 1A winner — and the league as a whole will have an impressive 10 teams playing sectional basketball this week.</p><h3>A handful of close games</h3><p>With just four one-possession games out of the 32 played, there wasn’t a whole lot of drama or last-second heroics in Friday’s 4A regional championship games.</p><p>The four barnburners included Palatine’s 44-42 win over Barrington, Stevenson’s one-point win over Buffalo Grove, and both Simeon beating Young and Normal getting by East Moline United by identical 55-54 scores.</p><p>While the results didn’t necessarily surprise as each of these teams were the higher seed, the actual final scores did. Here are a few scores from favorites that opened eyes:</p><p><b>Marist 82, Rich 54</b></p><p>The RedHawks, who I did pick to reach Champaign, went on the road and dismantled a Rich team that was playing well and was on its home floor.</p><p><b>Hersey 62, Rolling Meadows 36</b></p><p>These two met twice in the regular season and split two tight games.</p><p><b>Warren 70, Libertyville 65</b></p><p>There is a lot of familiarity between these two North Suburban Conference teams. But after losing twice in the regular season by 17 and 33 points, Libertyville hung in there and put a real scare into one of the state’s best teams.</p><h3>More notes</h3><ul id="rte-d8dd9852-164d-11f1-97d3-a70fcbcb7cae"><li>Stevenson hasn’t lost a regional game since 2018. Now the Patriots seek out their first sectional title since 2019.<br></li><li>The recent Palatine run is one that keeps adding to the record books. The latest — a school record 29 wins — came in its 44-42 win Friday night over Barrington. The record win total of 28 was previously set just two years ago.<br></li><li>Kaneland’s record remains unblemished at 32-0. That’s now 64 wins in two seasons and an average of 29 wins a year over the past four seasons. Impressive. The Knights took care of business in beating Burlington Central 70-52 behind Marcus Cocroft’s 30 points. They are two wins away from capturing back-to-back sectional championships.<br></li><li>The results of the Class 3A Hinsdale South Sectional left the following seeds still alive: No. 3, No. 5, No. 7 and No. 8. St. Francis, the No. 7 seed, took out top-seeded Joliet Catholic and the No. 2 seed, Oak Forest, lost to No. 8 Providence 39-36. Now No. 5 Lemont faces St. Francis, a team it beat back in December, while Providence and No. 3 seed Wheaton Academy meet in the other sectional semifinal.<br></li><li>Reviewing the 3A and 4A regional picks. The record in regional title game picks from Friday night in Class 4A: 28-4. The misses include Wheaton-Warrenville South, Simeon, Yorkville and Peoria Richwoods. The Class 3A picks were exactly the same at 28-4 with the misses being Lake Forest, Lincoln, Crystal Lake South and Providence.</li></ul>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: At least 6 killed in weekend gun violence citywide
entries.summary: <p>At least six men, including one who was gunned down during a gender reveal party, were killed in shootings throughout Chicago from 5 p.m. Friday to 5 a.m. Monday, officials confirmed.</p><p>Five of the six homicides happened Saturday in Chatham, Albany Park, Chicago Lawn, Bridgeport and Auburn Gresham. <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-9d755b92-1643-11f1-9174-a7fc7abdbda0"><li>Carlos Navarro Perez, 45, was fatally shot in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/02/boy-15-shot-in-woodlawn-drive-by" target="_blank" >one of multiple drive-by shootings Sunday night</a>, Chicago police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. Navarro Perez was standing outside in 3900 block of West 62nd Street in West Lawn around 8:30 p.m. Sunday and was shot in the head, authorities said. He was pronounced dead at 9:04 a.m.<br></li><li>A male was walking near the sidewalk in the<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/28/person-killed-in-chatham-drive-by-shooting" target="_blank" > 7900 block of South Ingleside Avenue</a> in Chatham around 5:30 p.m. Saturday when someone inside a car shot at him, striking him multiple times, officials said. He was taken to University of Chicago Medical Center, where he was pronounced dead, police said.<br></li><li>About half an hour earlier, Michael Rounds Jr., 19, was inside a home in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/28/man-19-killed-in-auburn-gresham-shooting" target="_blank" >7700 block of South Racine Avenue</a> at a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/02/man-19-fatally-shot-at-gender-reveal-party-in-auburn-gresham-police" target="_blank" >gender reveal party when someone in a passing car fired shots </a>and struck him in the head, officials said. He was pronounced dead at the scene.<br></li><li>In Albany Park, a man was killed and three others were wounded around 3 p.m. Saturday in the basement of a home in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/28/3-shot-1-fatally-in-albany-park-basement" target="_blank" >4900 block of North Harding Avenue</a>, officials said. An unidentified 24-year-old man suffered multiple gunshot wounds and was pronounced dead at the scene and two men, 29 and 34, were wounded and taken to Swedish Hospital in fair condition. <br></li><li>About 10 minutes earlier, two 40-year-old men were in an alley in the 300 block of West 29th Street in Armour Square when someone approached and shot both of them, police said. Both men were taken to Stroger Hospital initially in critical condition. One of the victims — identified as Carlos Alcantar — later died shortly after 9 p.m. Saturday, according to the medical examiner's office. <br></li><li>Emmanuel I. Aguiniga, 30, was fatally shot in Chicago Lawn around 3 a.m. Saturday in the 3200 block of 62nd Street when a sliver sedan approached and someone inside shot him multiple times while he stood outside, authorities said. He was pronounced dead at the scene. </li></ul><p>Police have not reported arrests in any of the attacks. </p><p>Detectives are investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Voters turn out across Chicago's 50 wards, suburbs ahead of March 17 primary
entries.summary: <p>Andersonville resident Anthony Hamilton stopped by the voting supersite location in the Loop Monday to drop off his mail-in ballot. He said he likes to fill out his ballot early to make sure his vote counts.</p><p>“I live in the ninth district and just there's 10 people running for the ninth district. And so right now it feels really important to have somebody who feels like they're speaking to as many issues as that you, like, are concerned about as possible.”</p><p>Hamilton, 33, was one of the many voters across the Chicago area turning out to vote, as locations in all 50 wards and in 55 locations in suburban Cook County opened ahead of the March 17 primary election. Additional early voting sites also opened in the collar counties.</p><p>Marisel Hernandez, the chairwoman of the Chicago Board of Election Commissioners, was joined by Cook County Clerk Monica Gordon for a news conference at the supersite to update voters ahead of the primary. Officials said that they urged voters to vote early to avoid issues.</p><p>"You never know how the weather will be in the beginning of March, or what circumstances may come your way preventing you from voting as you originally planned," Hernandez said. "We are urging Chicago voters to vote early and enjoy the peace of mind that comes with getting your civic duty out of the way."</p><p>Bobbi Ball, of Streeterville, came by the supersite to drop off her mail in ballot on Monday. Issues most important to her included affordability, healthcare and employment.</p><p>“I think we would like to go to the grocery store and to be able to afford to pay for the food that's in it, so that we could eat," Ball, 75, said. "It would be good for people who have lost their health insurance to get that back... And it would be good to pay attention to some of the errors that are facing our country now.”</p><p>Rochelle Hamilton, of Hyde Park, voted in person at the South Side YMCA in Woodlawn because of skepticism about mail in voting.</p><p>“Prior to this, I think the past 20 years, I voted by mail. But I'm not sure I trust the system anymore. I'd rather do it in person,” she said.</p><p>Ahead of the primary, Hamilton said she is most concerned about finding candidates that don’t take money from AIPAC.</p><p>“To be perfectly honest, I vote for all anti AIPAC candidates. That's what's most important to me, because I think a lot of the troubles we're having right now in foreign policy and even domestic are because of our allegiance to a foreign country,” she said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/19/rep-jan-schakowsky-withdrawal-donna-miller-endorsement-2nd-congressional-district-aipac-support" >Schakowsky withdraws Donna Miller endorsement in 2nd Congressional District over AIPAC support</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/23/super-pac-influence-congressional-primary-races-illinois" >Frustrated Illinois congressional candidates call super PAC influence ‘buying a seat’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>And in suburban Berwyn, the first thing on Danny Vilchez’s mind as he went vote at Berwyn Recreation Monday was affordability.</p><p>“Money has been the biggest issue my family has had for I don’t even know how long,” Vilchez said. “We were all told everything would be cheaper. … Everything has been the same way, if not worse.”</p><p>The 22-year-old had to transfer colleges last year when money troubles made his first school no longer viable. With $13,000 in loans and plans to pursue a masters in public policy in the fall, he said he lost faith in those who won in 2024 to deliver on promises of a more affordable country.</p><p>While he identified as a Republican when he voted for the first time in 2022, he has since shifted leftward, in part because of immigration policy. He believes there should be stronger pathways to citizenship.</p><p>“My family is Hispanic, so it felt like we weren’t their target demographic anymore,” Vilchez said. “[And] I grew up in Chicago, so I got very upset seeing the raids in Little Village specifically.”</p><p>Over 170,500 applications to vote by mail had been submitted in Chicago and over 26,000 votes have already been cast, Hernandez said. </p><p>Compared to the 2022 primary election, officials said they are seeing an increase in the number of votes. At this point in the June 2022 primary, she said about 11,000 votes had been cast.</p><p>"Voters look at the climate, in our country, and in our state and city, at the time of election," Hernandez said. </p><p>She also attributed the increase in votes to the number of open positions in which incumbents are not running for re-election. </p><p>Voters <a class="Link" href="https://www.suntimeswire.com/2026/02/12/voters-cast-ballots-at-loop-supersite-as-early-voting-kicks-off-everythings-at-stake" target="_blank" >have been able to vote early</a> at the Chicago Board of Elections office at 69 W. Washington St. and at the downtown supersite at 137 S. State St. since February 12.</p><p>"No matter where you live in the city, whether it's a site closest to your home, to school, or your place of work, you can pick any early voting site to cast your ballot," Hernandez said.</p><p>Find a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/how-to-vote-illinois-primary" >full list of early voting sites here</a>. Voters who want to vote by mail can still sign up for a mail-in ballot. The deadline to apply is 5 p.m. March 12. </p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: White Sox' Braden Montgomery confident he will find Fame
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> right fielder Braden Montgomery wasn’t trying to come off as a cocky kid, but his confidence is obviously through the roof.</p><p>Asked Sunday by the Sun-Times if his ultimate goal is to make the White Sox this spring, Montgomery responded with this mind-bending answer:</p><p>“My overall goal is to make the Hall of Fame,” Montgomery said matter-of-factly after he homered and tripled against the Cubs at Mesa’s Sloan Park in his first taste of Chicago’s ancient baseball rivalry. “I’ll take it a game at a time, and we’ll see what happens at the end of it.”</p><p>Oh, really?</p><p>“That’s awesome to hear,” Sox manager Will Venable said Monday at Camelback Ranch when told about Montgomery’s comment. “He’s one of those guys; if that’s what he thinks and that’s what he’s working for, you just want to support him so he can do that.”</p><p>Montgomery was advised that Tony Gwynn’s goal right from the beginning of his major league career with the Padres in 1982 was to reach 3,000 hits, one hit at a time. Gwynn retired in 2001 with 3,141 hits, and a .338 batting average, the highest then since Ted Williams retired in 1960 at .344. It still is. That led to Gwynn’s 2007 induction into the National Baseball Hall of Fame along with Cal Ripken Jr.</p><p>“See what happens when you’re goal oriented?” Montgomery said when told about Gwynn. “You take it a pitch at a time, a second at a time.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">MLB Pipeline's No. 36 overall prospect leaves the yard!<br><br>Braden Montgomery has his first <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/SpringTraining?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#SpringTraining</a> homer 💪 <a href="https://t.co/QKvgqhtPK3">pic.twitter.com/QKvgqhtPK3</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2028207067547255278?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 1, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Montgomery hasn’t played a game yet in the major leagues and is ticketed to start the regular season again in the minors. But he said being elected to Cooperstown has always been a dream of his since growing up as a kid in Des Moines, Iowa. He’s now 22, but will turn 23 on April 16. A first-round pick, and 12th overall, by the Boston Red Sox in the 2024 draft of first-year players, Montgomery was a key element for the White Sox in the Garrett Crochet trade.</p><p>“Ever since I watched Derek Jeter play I’ve wanted to be in the big leagues,” he said of the Yankees Hall of Famer. “I’d say about 10 or 11 years old I knew I wanted to be the best playing baseball. Obviously that comes with eventually donning a gold jacket. Obviously, it’s not even Day 1 of my big-league career yet. I take it day-by-day. If I do that I think the results will be something I can live with.”</p><p>His Sunday in a 5-1 White Sox win over the Cubs was obviously something he could live with. He went 2-for-3 with the homer, triple, a run scored and an RBI bringing his Cactus League batting average to .357 and his OPS to a gaudy 1.214.</p><p>His homer with two out in the second inning came off veteran Cubs left-hander Shota Imanaga, one of three the Japanese hurler allowed in his 2 2/3 inning stint.</p><p>“That was super cool,” Montgomery said. “Being able to see these guys on the diamond you’ve seen on TV. To further compartmentalize it I’m part of that now. To be able to share the field with him is really cool. That’s why I put in the work every day and want to continue to share the field with them by getting results.”</p><p>The triple off Luke Little to lead off the sixth soared over right fielder Michael Conforto, who as a fading veteran, is trying to maintain a roster a spot in the big leagues in his first to tour with the Cubs.</p><p>Did Montgomery hit that triple better than the homer?</p><p>“I don’t know. I guess we’ll find out when we got those hitting reports,” he said. “But those triples always feel better. You get to go off to the races. It’s all eyes on you and you get to see what you can do.”</p><p>No wonder, the professed future Hall Of Famer had a perpetual smile on his face. It seems like he’s having a great time.</p><p>“I try to be the same way every because this truly is a blessing,” he said. “Playing baseball and being able to see all these fans? It’s just a beautiful thing.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: War is much easier to enter than to exit
entries.summary: <p>America has fought many wars. And built many war memorials.</p><p>Wandering around Washington, D.C., I made a point to stop by the biggies — the Vietnam Veterans Memorial, No. 1 in my book, for breaking war's symbolic stranglehold on imagined glory. A black granite gash in the earth featuring not eagles but the names of the 58,318 American dead.</p><p>The Korean War Veterans Memorial, a night patrol of 19 stainless steel figures, in ponchos against the cold rain, faces etched with stress and fatigue, frozen in mid-stride. Even the sprawling, soulless World War II Memorial.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ad0000" name="module-ad0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>The World War I Memorial wasn't on my radar. Until I found myself next to it, at the corner of 15th and Pennsylvania Avenue. A few extra steps, and there was Sabin Howard's epic sculpture "A Soldier's Journey." Starting with a doughboy taking leave of his wife and daughter, charging into combat, men around him killed, wounded, with a homecoming at the end.</p><p>World War I is a stark reminder of the greasy slope of war — what started with an assassinated Austrian archduke exploded into fighting across the globe, ending 31 years later — historians consider World War II an extension of World War I, after a 21-year intermission to raise a new generation of cannon fodder.</p><p>War between the United States and Iran commenced Saturday. It'll end... nobody knows, of course. We assume it'll be a few tightly contained airstrikes, like last summer.</p><p>But then war always seems quick, at the outset, with the boys hurrahing down to the recruiting office to sign up, worried the action will be over before Christmas. The Russians, don't forget, rolled into Ukraine four years ago, expecting to be in Kyiv in a few days. They're still fighting, having lost an estimated 200,000 men.</p><p> When World War I broke out in 1914, President Woodrow Wilson, promised not to get involved. "He kept us out of war" was his re-election slogan in 1916. "No new wars," Donald Trump echoed in 2024.</p><p>Both promises worked. Both were broken. Both with reason. Iran is the worst sponsor of international terrorism — Hamas could have never pulled off the Oct. 7 attacks without Iran's enthusiastic backing — making it impossible not to welcome the elimination of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. "It's been mass terror, and we're not going to put up with it any longer," Trump said.</p><p>You go, Mr. President. Add it to the list of Trump successes, along with elimination of the penny. Whether that counterbalances scuttling voting rights, well, you decide.</p><p>We're attacking Iran now... why exactly? To destroy its capacity to produce nuclear weapons? Sounds laudable. But also very... familiar. Didn't we just do that?</p><p>“A spectacular military success” Trump said after the strikes last July. “Iran’s key enrichment facilities have been completely and totally obliterated.”</p><p>"Totally, again, obliterated," Trump said Saturday. Maybe it'll stick this time. We're also calling for regime change. That seems naive — how well did that work in Afghanistan? Or to return to World War I: remember the regime Germany ended up with after Versailles. We liked the Nazis even less.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-520000" name="image-520000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1545048/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5704x3201+0+301/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fc6%2F4664b4c24623881bfda3e2c8fe98%2Fap26060537702769.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A black plume of smoke rises from a warehouse at the industrial area of Sharjah City in the United Arab Emirates following reports of Iranian strikes in Dubai, United Arab Emirates Sunday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Altaf Qadri/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The new war seems doubly troubling when you look at our own besieged democracy.</p><p>"It's domestic politics," said Timothy Snyder, whose books such as "On Tyranny" explore the rise of repressive regimes and how we risk becoming one of them. "The need to create some kind of foreign conflict so Trump can say we have to do special things around the elections. I honestly think its as simple as that."</p><p>Alas, World War I has lessons here, too — the government cracked down on dissidents, jailing pacifists, deporting foreign nationals who dared oppose the bloodshed.</p><p>War is a gamble. When you win big early on — kidnapping Maduro, killing Khamenei — you can fall under the spell of imagining it's a game you can win.</p><p>It could be argued that to start a war is to lose it. That's the sense you get, standing before any war memorial. Though now might not be the time to go to Washington — I know people who were afraid to step foot in Chicago Sunday, worried about blundering into some ad hoc Iranian counterstrike.</p><p>But if you do go to D.C., definitely visit the memorial to Vietnam, that bloody bog that took years to extricate ourselves from. And do seek out the World War I Memorial. The 58-foot wide sculpture is a true masterpiece, including a bit of the glory that some still itch to attach to war.</p><p>Here is how the National Park Service web site puts it: "At the center our hero calls his comrades into battle, illustrating the famous American battle cry from Belleau Wood: 'Come on, you sons of bitches, do you want to live forever?'"</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: DePaul faculty, students call on university to reverse decision to close campus art museum
entries.summary: <p>Several DePaul professors have written an <a class="Link" href="https://openletter.earth/letter-opposing-the-closing-of-dpam-f02e5d5a?limit=0" target="_blank" ><u>open letter</u></a> calling on the university to reconsider its decision to close its art museum. The university confirmed last week it plans to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/museums-zoos/2026/02/26/depaul-art-museum-closing-in-june-amid-campus-layoffs-financial-troubles" target="_blank" ><u>close its museum permanently on June 30.</u></a></p><p>Six faculty members organized the letter published online on Saturday. As of press time, more than 1,500 students, faculty, staff and alumni have added their names to the letter.</p><p>The museum is considered an important local venue for underrepresented artists. Its collection of more than 4,000 objects includes photography by artists including Andy Warhol and Chicagoans Dawoud Bey and Paul D’Amato. The museum also has a considerable holding of West African objects and Latino art.</p><p>The open letter is signed by arts leaders around Chicago, including the collections manager at the Museum of Contemporary Art, the curator at Old Dominion University’s Barry Art Museum and librarians from the School of the Art Institute.</p><p>The statement asks the university to reconsider the art museum’s closure on two grounds: “the basis of the internal and substantive pedagogical value that the museum’s collection” and “the external contribution that the museum makes toward bolstering DePaul’s profile as an institution so committed to the arts that it built an ‘arts corridor,’ which links DPAM to the recently constructed Music School, Arts and Letters building, and Theater School.”</p><p>Sean Kirkland, a philosophy professor at DePaul and principal organizer of the letter-writing campaign, said he was “shocked” and “didn’t anticipate” the museum’s closure.</p><p>The news comes two months after DePaul University<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/15/depaul-university-layoff-cuts-chicago-lincoln-park" target="_blank" ><u> laid off 114 employees</u></a> in December.</p><p>“We'd heard, of course, that there were financial pressures and that kind of thing, but we really didn't expect that closure would be the response to those pressures,” Kirkland said.</p><p>The goal of the letter is to “try to generate some kind of public outcry about the closure and see if we could reverse the administration's decision,” he said.</p><p>While the university has not yet responded to the petition, the group plans to formally present it to three top administrators this week: President Rob Manuel, Provost Salma Ghanem and Executive V.P. Sherri Sidler.</p><p>Kirkland said the museum is a loss for the students in his environmental philosophy class, who typically visit when there are exhibitions that relate to the natural world.</p><p>“Many of us look for every opportunity to introduce our students to the museum space and to the collection,” Kirkland said. “We kind of want to confront our students with things that push them outside the sphere of utility and cause them to think in new ways and experience the world in new ways. And that seems to me precisely what art does.”</p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Los manifestantes anti ICE golpean a Trump en la corte de Chicago, pero todo sigue teniendo un costo
entries.summary: <p>Las fotografías de Ray Collins y Jocelyne Robledo bajo custodia federal están a sólo una búsqueda de Google de distancia, tan fáciles de encontrar como las publicaciones en redes sociales que los llaman “alborotadores armados” e “individuos trastornados”.</p><p>Las autoridades arrestaron a la pareja de Englewood hace cinco meses fuera de un centro de procesamiento de inmigración en Broadview, sólo unas semanas después de que el presidente Donald Trump lanzara una agresiva campaña de deportaciones en el área de Chicago en septiembre pasado.</p><p>Sus casos de asalto se desmoronaron rápidamente cuando un <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/08/feds-dropping-charges-against-couple-who-lawfully-carried-guns-outside-ice-facility" target="_blank" >gran jurado se negó a imputar</a>. Collins encontró que estaba siendo llamado “de todo, desde asesino hasta depredador”. Y ahora tiene una advertencia para posibles empleadores que puedan intentar buscarlo: “Fui arrestado por un delito grave”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d40000" name="module-d40000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Es casi vergonzoso”, le dijo al Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Pero él es resiliente.</p><p>“Conozco mi carácter, que esas cosas nunca se sostendrán”, destacó Collins. “No pueden manchar lo que he hecho en mi comunidad. No pueden manchar quién soy como persona”.</p><p>Collins y Robledo están entre un número creciente de manifestantes que han tenido que recoger los pedazos después de enfrentarse a la administración de Trump en las cortes y ganar.</p><p>Las acusaciones por crímenes no relacionados con la inmigración vinculados al Operativo Midway Blitz se han desintegrado a un ritmo alarmante en la Corte Federal Dirksen desde octubre. Los casos contra 17 de los 32 conocidos demandados <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >ya han colapsado</a>, incluidos los de Collins y Robledo.</p><p>Pero todo esto sigue teniendo un costo. Una acusación federal es intimidante. Conlleva la amenaza de tiempo en prisión. Historias dañinas se difunden en línea. Las libertades a menudo están restringidas, incluso en casos menores. Se necesitan abogados, aunque defensores federales hayan salvado a las personas de grandes facturas legales.</p><p>Luego está la cárcel.</p><p>Los 17 demandados liberados pasaron un total de 150 días bajo custodia federal, según muestran los registros. Eso no incluye los 35 días, y contando, que Juan Espinoza Martínez ha estado detenido por las autoridades de inmigración <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/23/jurado-rechaza-complot-de-asesinato-de-greg-bovino-y-absuelve-a-hombre-de-chicago" target="_blank" >desde que fue absuelto</a> por supuestamente haber intentado asesinar a Gregory Bovino, el agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza que se convirtió en el rostro del operativo de deportación.</p><p>Eso sin mencionar el costo físico y emocional soportado por Marimar Martínez, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >la mujer a la que un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza le disparó cinco veces</a>en el lado suroeste a principios de octubre.</p><p>Las apuestas no son tan altas en todos los casos. Jackie Guataquira, cuyo caso menor de cuatro meses fue recientemente desestimado, dijo que nunca pasó una noche en la cárcel y no tuvo que presentarse físicamente en la corte. Pero la acusación le impidió visitar a su familia en México, dijo Guataquira.</p><p>Esto demuestra “que quieren venir por las personas a todos los niveles”, le dijo al Sun-Times.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ADy5XJc9b-Y?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="This Chicago couple fought charges brought by the Trump administration — and won"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>“Realmente están tratando de quitarle la vida a la gente, deportarlos de este país, o a un nivel más menor, pintar a las personas que están protestando por justicia y enfrentándose a esto como ‘terroristas’”, dijo. "Están llenando las redes sociales con las fotos de estas personas por esto, sólo para alejar a los demás de poder protestar”.</p><h3>‘Tu vida se pone de cabeza’</h3><p>Se espera que los fiscales federales presenten cargos sólo cuando puedan probarlos ante un jurado más allá de una duda razonable. Normalmente, presumen un índice de condena de alrededor del 90%. Pero el Departamento de Justicia de Trump ha sufrido una serie de pérdidas de alto perfil vinculadas a su campaña nacional de inmigración, incluyendo una reciente <a class="Link" href="https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-02-06/federal-public-defenders-winning" target="_blank" >serie de seis casos perdidos</a> en Los Ángeles.</p><p>En Chicago, la oficina del <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/05/30/andrew-boutros-interim-us-attorney-chicago-judges-role-pam-bondi" target="_blank" >fiscal Andrew Boutros</a> está viendo una tasa de condena en el mejor de los casos de aproximadamente del 47% en tales casos. Eso asumiendo que obtengan una condena contra cada uno de los 15 demandados restantes, y que no se presenten cargos adicionales.</p><p>Boutros dijo que muchos de los casos se presentaron como resultado de “arrestos reactivos” en el campo. Algunos de ellos eran más adecuados para la corte estatal, dijo. Otros fueron desestimados por la preocupación de que los jurados rechazarían los cargos incluso cuando se prueben, o por factores atenuantes como la salud mental.</p><p>“Nuestra gracia no debe confundirse como un reflejo de la fortaleza de un caso particular o si un cargo debería haberse presentado”, dijo Boutros en un comunicado. “Es un testimonio de nuestra visión de que el papel del fiscal es buscar justicia y no sólo condenar.</p><p>“Decidimos casos basándonos en hechos, leyes y equidades sin tener en cuenta la actitud de escepticismo desdeñosa sostenida por un cierto grupo de críticos”.</p><p>La fiscal general Pam Bondi <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/03/28/former-prosecutor-andrew-boutros-named-chicagos-interim-u-s-attorney" target="_blank" >nombró inicialmente a Boutros</a> para su trabajo de manera temporal a principios de 2025. Los jueces de la Corte de Distrito de Chicago luego <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2025/07/25/chicagos-federal-judges-choose-andrew-boutros-as-top-prosecutor" target="_blank" >lo eligieron para servir</a> de manera más permanente una vez que expiró el nombramiento de Bondi.</p><p>Laurie Levenson, profesora de la Facultad de Derecho de Loyola en Los Ángeles, dijo que hay “consecuencias enormes” cuando alguien es acusado de un delito federal. Eso es cierto incluso si la acusación es abandonada, dijo Levenson.</p><p>"Tu vida se pone de cabeza cuando te acusan de un caso federal”, dijo Levenson.</p><p>Pero el mayor costo, dijo, es para “la credibilidad del sistema de justicia”.</p><h3>Cargos desestimados, pero el daño está hecho</h3><p>Las autoridades arrestaron a Collins, de 32 años, y a Robledo, de 30, el 27 de septiembre afuera de la instalación del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en Broadview, que se convirtió en un centro de intensas protestas.</p><p>Tres días antes, un tirador abrió fuego en una instalación de ICE en Dallas. Las emociones estaban en un nivel alto. Sin nombrar a la pareja, Bovino se refirió a Collins y Robledo en línea como “dos individuos trastornados, armados con pistolas”. Usuarios en X compartieron <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/CMDROpAtLargeCA/status/1972684789028171930" target="_blank" >su publicación en redes sociales</a> 278 veces.</p><p>“No lo descubras de la manera difícil”, escribió Bovino.</p><p>Collins y Robledo nunca enfrentaron cargos relacionados con las armas, que estaban portando legalmente y nunca exhibieron.</p><p>En cambio, una acusación criminal alegaba que Robledo ignoró órdenes y se opuso cuando la policía les dijo a las personas que se movieran.</p><p>Una vez que los oficiales comenzaron a luchar con Robledo, Collins supuestamente les gritó que se alejaran y avanzaron contra ellos. Los federales dijeron que Collins se resistió ante los agentes e hirió el dedo pulgar de uno. Collins le dijo al Sun-Times que “no inició” el altercado y “no agredió a nadie”.</p><p>Los fiscales luego buscaron que la pareja fuera detenida. El juez de magistrados Gabriel Fuentes dictó que Robledo no calificaba para la detención. Collins sí, debido a la lesión del agente, y el abogado de Collins insistió en una audiencia inmediata.</p><p>El juez ordenó la detención de Collins. Cuestionó el juicio de Collins al llevar un arma a una protesta de ICE, “particularmente dada la reciente historia de tácticas de aplicación agresivas de ICE mientras estaban fuertemente armados”.</p><p>Robledo dijo que Collins, que es negro, fue retenido en la cárcel más tiempo “simplemente por el color de su piel”. Collins dijo que sospecha que el juez “subconscientemente” asumió que “yo portaba un arma y era un individuo peligroso debido a mi presentación en corte, mi apariencia, y el área de donde soy”.</p><p>En su orden escrita, Fuentes dijo que la solicitud de defensa para una audiencia inmediata lo dejó sin un informe que podría haber mencionado el empleo de Collins y la ausencia de antecedentes criminales, así como su contexto personal. La decisión “fue una llamada extremadamente cercana”, escribió el juez.</p><p>Fuentes predijo que un juez revisor podría aprovechar “un registro más completo”.</p><p>Tres días después, el juez del distrito Sunil Harjani ordenó la liberación de Collins con esa información adicional en mano.</p><p>Luego, el 8 de octubre, los federales desestimaron los cargos. Un gran jurado se había negado a imputar, en una rara reprimenda a los fiscales federales.</p><p>El tiempo pasado en la cárcel fue “estresante”, dijo Collins.</p><p>“No porque tuviera miedo de estar en prisión federal”, explicó. “Sino sólo por el estrés de saber que no puedo cuidar de mis mascotas, de mi familia. Que el sistema de justicia no funciona incluso cuando no has hecho nada malo”.</p><p>Desde entonces Robledo ha sido reacia a unirse a protestas en Broadview. Como alguien que ha estado involucrada en manifestaciones desde los 13 años, Robledo dijo que eso es “transformador”, pero insistió en que no es “desgarrador”.</p><p>“Hace más difícil para ti hacer cualquier cosa”, dijo Robledo sobre la acusación. “Ese es el punto, ¿verdad?.</p><p>“Pero decidí hace mucho tiempo que eso no me iba a pasar”.</p><p>Desde entonces, Fuentes ha <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/20/unusual-and-possibly-unprecedented-judge-calls-out-chicago-feds-as-midway-blitz-cases-fall-apart" target="_blank" >cuestionado algunos de los cargos</a> presentados en medio del Operativo Midway Blitz, incluidos los de Collins y Robledo. Lo hizo en un dictamen emitido a finales de noviembre, después de que una serie de casos ante él se desmoronó.</p><p>El juez escribió que había “obtenido confirmación jurada de que el video respaldaba” las afirmaciones de los federales contra Collins y Robledo. Aún así, escribió, “el gran jurado se negó a encontrar causa probable”.</p><p>“Eso es un evento preocupante para un juez de magistrados”, escribió Fuentes.</p><p>Agregó, “Cualquier fiscal federal responsable sabe que los cargos federales, o cualquier acción por parte del fiscal dirigida a la ciudadanía, deben llevarse a cabo con el mayor cuidado”.</p><h3>‘Niveles de represión’</h3><p>Martínez se ha convertido en la más prominente de los 17 demandados liberados del Operativo Midway Blitz en Chicago. Testificó recientemente <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-testifica-en-washington-pide-a-funcionarios-que-dejen-de-llamarla-terrorista-domestica" target="_blank" >ante miembros demócratas del Congreso</a>. También asistió al discurso sobre el Estado de la Unión de Trump el martes como invitada del representante demócrata de Illinois, Jesús “Chuy” García.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e50000" name="image-e50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" alt="Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago que recibió cinco disparos de agentes federales de inmigración en octubre, testifica en Washington, D.C. en febrero." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f75135b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1436x806+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F70%2Fa5624f17494fb3ae86ae8b8b90ab%2Fmarimar-testifying.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d6837e1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1436x806+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F70%2Fa5624f17494fb3ae86ae8b8b90ab%2Fmarimar-testifying.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f75135b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1436x806+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F70%2Fa5624f17494fb3ae86ae8b8b90ab%2Fmarimar-testifying.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago que recibió cinco disparos de agentes federales de inmigración en octubre, testifica en Washington, D.C. en febrero.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>(Captura de pantalla del Sen. Richard Blumenthal de la transmisión en vivo)</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Pero a principios de octubre, se encontró recuperándose de sus heridas de bala durante una noche en el Centro Correccional Metropolitano (MCC) federal. La asistente de maestra de Montessori de 30 años dijo que todo fue como de "película".</p><p>Dijo que aprendió a no confiar en nadie allí, incluyendo los guardias.</p><p>“Recuerdo que solía escuchar a los reclusos de arriba golpeando, haciendo ritmos en las paredes”, dijo Martínez en una reciente entrevista. “Fue surrealista”.</p><p>Martínez pasó 46 días acusada de agredir al agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza que le disparó, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/11/agentes-dijeron-es-hora-de-ser-agresivos-en-video-que-contradice-las-afirmaciones-contra-marimar-martinez" target="_blank" >Charles Exum</a>. Luego, el 20 de noviembre, los fiscales federales <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/21/desestiman-el-caso-contra-mujer-a-la-que-le-disparo-agente-de-la-patrulla-fronteriza-en-brighton-park" target="_blank" >desestimaron abruptamente los cargos</a> en su contra y los de otro hombre, Anthony Ian Santos Ruiz.</p><p>Los funcionarios de Seguridad Nacional etiquetaron a Martínez como “terrorista doméstica” y han rechazado rescindir la afirmación, incluso cuando parece que una publicación en redes sociales sobre Collins y Robledo ha desaparecido.</p><p>Los oficiales de ICE no respondieron a una solicitud de comentario sobre Collins y Robledo.</p><p>Hasta ahora, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/23/jurado-rechaza-complot-de-asesinato-de-greg-bovino-y-absuelve-a-hombre-de-chicago" target="_blank" >sólo un caso del Operativo Midway Blitz ha llegado a juicio</a>. Los federales acusaron a Espinoza Martínez, de 37 años, de ofrecer $10,000 por el asesinato de Bovino. Espinoza Martínez pasó 109 días en la cárcel, mucho más que cualquier otro demandado del Operativo Midway Blitz que haya sido liberado.</p><p>Su acusación giró en torno a un mensaje de Snapchat que envió a un conocido que supuestamente ofrecía $2,000 por la captura de Bovino y $10,000 por su cabeza. Espinoza Martínez es ciudadano mexicano y padre que ha vivido en Chicago durante 30 años y no tiene antecedentes penales, según los registros.</p><p>Les dijo a los agentes que no tenía la intención de que nadie llevara a cabo realmente el asesinato.</p><p>El 22 de enero, el jurado declaró a Espinoza Martínez no culpable. Pero Seguridad Nacional <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/24/juan-espinoza-martinez-bovino-murder-plot-ice-custody" target="_blank" >lo detuvo al día siguiente</a>, mientras era liberado del MCC. Aún está detenido en el Centro de Justicia del Condado de Clay en Indiana, después de que un juez de inmigración determinó que representaba un “potencial” peligro para la comunidad.</p><p>Espinoza Martínez sólo apareció en el radar de los agentes federales debido al mensaje de Snapchat que envió, dijeron los fiscales durante el juicio.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c90000" name="image-c90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490"
